sentiment.rationale: A fierce oracle of divine judgment upon all nations, especially Edom, with imagery of cosmic destruction and desolation.
A radiant vision of the ransomed returning to Zion with singing, as the desert blossoms and sorrow flees away.
Rabshakeh's threatening speech against Jerusalem, challenging Hezekiah's trust in the Lord.
Hezekiah's prayer and Isaiah's prophecy of Sennacherib's defeat, culminating in the angel destroying 185,000 Assyrians.
Hezekiah's mortal illness, tearful prayer, miraculous healing, and his psalm reflecting on suffering and divine deliverance.
Hezekiah foolishly shows Babylon's envoys all his treasures; Isaiah prophesies the coming Babylonian captivity.
The great comfort chapter — declares God's incomparable power, that those who wait upon the Lord shall renew their strength and mount up with wings as eagles.
God reassures Israel as His chosen servant, promising strength and help, rebuking the nations and their idols.
Introduces the Servant of the Lord who will bring justice to the Gentiles, followed by a call to sing a new song.
God declares He will redeem Israel, calling them by name, promising to be with them through fire and water.
God will pour out His Spirit on Israel's seed; a satirical attack on idol-making contrasts with the Lord as Redeemer.
Cyrus is named as God's anointed instrument; every knee shall bow and every tongue confess to the Lord.
Contrast between Babylon's helpless idols that must be carried and the Lord who carries Israel from birth to old age.
Pronouncement of judgment upon Babylon, the virgin daughter, for her cruelty, pride, and sorceries.
God rebukes Israel's stubbornness while declaring new things; peace could have been as a river if they had hearkened.
The Servant is called from the womb to be a light to the Gentiles; God declares He will never forget Zion — engraved on the palms of His hands.
The Servant sets his face like a flint and will not be confounded; those who walk in darkness and kindle their own fire are warned.
Calls the righteous to look to Abraham; the redeemed shall return with singing, and everlasting joy upon their heads.
Zion awakens and puts on beautiful garments; how beautiful upon the mountains are the feet of him that publisheth peace.
The Suffering Servant chapter — He is despised, rejected, wounded for our transgressions, bruised for our iniquities; with His stripes we are healed.
The barren woman sings; God promises to gather Israel with great mercies and establish an everlasting covenant of peace.
An invitation to come to the waters without money; God's word shall not return void; mountains and hills shall break forth in singing.
God's house shall be a house of prayer for all people; blind watchmen and dumb dogs are condemned for their negligence.
Denounces idolatry and spiritual adultery; yet God dwells with the contrite and humble and creates the fruit of the lips: Peace.
True fasting defined: loose the bands of wickedness, feed the hungry, clothe the naked; then light breaks forth as the morning.
Israel's sins have separated them from God; truth has fallen in the street; the Redeemer shall come to Zion.
Arise, shine, for thy light is come; the glory of the Lord is risen upon thee; nations shall come to thy light.
The Spirit of the Lord is upon me to preach good tidings, proclaim liberty to captives, and the year of the Lord's favor.
Zion's new name and glory; the Lord sets watchmen on Jerusalem's walls who shall never hold their peace.
The Lord treads the winepress of judgment alone; a prayer recalling God's past mercies and lamenting current desolation.
A plea for God to rend the heavens and come down; we are the clay and Thou our potter; all our righteousnesses are as filthy rags.
God creates new heavens and a new earth; the wolf and lamb feed together; former troubles are forgotten.
The Lord looks to the poor and contrite; Zion brings forth children in a day; all flesh shall come to worship before the Lord.

summaries.summary: A proclamation of the Lord's indignation against all nations, with Edom singled out for devastating judgment as the land becomes perpetual desolation.
A vision of millennial restoration where the desert blossoms, the blind see, the deaf hear, and the ransomed of the Lord return to Zion with everlasting joy.
Sennacherib's field commander Rabshakeh besieges Jerusalem and delivers a threatening speech, attempting to undermine Judah's trust in the Lord.
Hezekiah prays in the temple, Isaiah delivers God's answer promising Sennacherib's defeat, and an angel destroys 185,000 Assyrian soldiers in one night.
Hezekiah falls mortally ill, prays for healing, and is granted fifteen additional years; he writes a psalm of thanksgiving for his deliverance from death.
Hezekiah shows all his treasures to Babylonian envoys; Isaiah prophesies that Babylon will one day carry away everything and take his descendants captive.
God comforts His people, declaring His incomparable power over nations and idols; those who wait upon the Lord shall renew their strength and mount up with wings as eagles.
God summons the nations to judgment, reassures Israel as His chosen servant, and challenges the idols to declare future events, which they cannot do.
The Lord's Servant is introduced — gentle, just, a light to the Gentiles — followed by a new song of praise and a lament over blind and deaf Israel.
God promises to redeem and protect Israel through fire and flood, declaring He blots out their transgressions and calls them to witness that He alone is God.
God promises to pour out His Spirit on Israel's offspring; a satirical passage mocks idol-makers; the Lord names Cyrus as the one who will rebuild Jerusalem.
Cyrus is called God's anointed shepherd; God declares there is no other God, and every knee shall bow and every tongue swear allegiance to the Lord.
Babylon's idols Bel and Nebo are carried on beasts, while the Lord carries Israel from birth; God declares the end from the beginning.
A taunt against Babylon — once a queen, now humbled in the dust; her sorceries and enchantments will not save her from sudden destruction.
God rebukes Israel for stubbornness and idolatry, reveals new things, and laments that their peace could have been like a river had they obeyed.
The Servant of the Lord is called from the womb to restore Israel and be a light to the Gentiles; Zion's children are engraved on the palms of God's hands.
The Servant sets his face like a flint, trusting in the Lord despite being smitten and spat upon; those who walk in self-made light will lie down in sorrow.
God calls the righteous to look to Abraham and Sarah, promises comfort to Zion, and declares that the redeemed shall return with singing and everlasting joy.
Zion is commanded to awake and put on beautiful garments; how beautiful upon the mountains are the feet of him who publishes peace; the Servant is introduced as marred beyond recognition.
The Suffering Servant is despised and rejected, bearing griefs and carrying sorrows; He is wounded for our transgressions and bruised for our iniquities, making His soul an offering for sin.
The barren woman is told to sing and enlarge her tent; God promises to gather Israel with great mercies, establishing an everlasting covenant of peace.
An invitation to come to the waters freely without money; God's thoughts and ways are higher than man's; His word shall accomplish its purpose and not return void.
The Lord promises blessings to foreigners and eunuchs who keep the Sabbath; His house shall be called a house of prayer for all people; blind watchmen are condemned.
The righteous perish while the wicked practice idolatry and spiritual adultery; yet God dwells with the contrite spirit and creates peace for those who mourn.
True fasting is defined as loosing the bonds of wickedness, feeding the hungry, and clothing the naked; then light will break forth and God will guide continually.
Israel's iniquities have separated them from God; justice and truth have fallen; the Redeemer shall come to those in Zion who turn from transgression.
Zion's future glory — arise, shine, for thy light is come; nations and kings come to her brightness; the Lord will be an everlasting light.
The Spirit of the Lord anoints the Servant to preach good tidings to the meek, proclaim liberty to captives, and announce the acceptable year of the Lord.
God will not rest until Zion's righteousness shines as brightness; she receives a new name, and watchmen are set on Jerusalem's walls who never hold their peace.
The Lord comes from Edom with garments dipped in the blood of judgment; a prayer recalls God's past lovingkindness and pleads for Him to return and redeem.
A petition for God to rend the heavens and come down; the people confess they are the clay and God is the potter; their holy city lies waste.
God contrasts rebellious Israel with His faithful servants; He creates new heavens and a new earth where weeping ceases and the wolf and lamb feed together.
The Lord declares heaven is His throne; He looks to the poor and contrite in spirit; Zion brings forth a nation in a day; all flesh shall worship before Him.

crossReferences.note: The angel of the Lord destroys the Assyrian army, demonstrating God's power to deliver His people.
The voice crying in the wilderness to prepare the way of the Lord, applied to John the Baptist.
Every knee shall bow and every tongue confess — a universal acknowledgment of God's sovereignty.
Can a woman forget her sucking child? Nephi quotes this passage about God's unfailing love for Zion.
Awake and put on thy strength, O Zion — interpreted in the Doctrine and Covenants regarding the last days.
How beautiful upon the mountains are the feet of him that publisheth peace — quoted by Abinadi.
He is despised and rejected of men, a man of sorrows — Abinadi quotes the entire chapter.
God's ways are higher than man's ways — a theme echoed in Nephi's trust in divine purposes.
Jesus reads this passage in the synagogue at Nazareth, declaring it fulfilled in their hearing.
Imagery of garments red from treading the winepress — applied to Christ's second coming.

historicalContext.setting: Prophetic oracle — cosmic scope, Edom as archetype
Jerusalem, during Sennacherib's invasion of Judah
Jerusalem, the temple and Hezekiah's court
Prophetic courtroom scene among the nations
Prophetic vision of the Servant's mission
Prophetic oracle naming Cyrus of Persia
Prophetic rebuke and promise to Israel
Prophetic comfort to the faithful remnant
Prophetic vision of Zion's restoration and the Servant
Prophetic address on Sabbath and inclusiveness
Prophetic rebuke of idolatry in Israel
Prophetic vision of Zion's millennial glory
Prophetic vision of new heavens and new earth

verses.reason: Declares the day of the Lord's vengeance and the year of recompenses for the controversy of Zion.
The wilderness and the solitary place shall be glad; the desert shall rejoice and blossom as the rose.
The ransomed of the Lord shall return to Zion with singing and everlasting joy upon their heads.
The angel of the Lord smites 185,000 Assyrians in one night — one of the most dramatic divine interventions in the OT.
God hears Hezekiah's prayer and adds fifteen years to his life.
Isaiah prophesies that all Hezekiah's treasures will be carried to Babylon — foreshadowing the exile.
The voice of him that crieth in the wilderness — a prophecy of John the Baptist preparing the Lord's way.
They that wait upon the Lord shall renew their strength; they shall mount up with wings as eagles.
Fear thou not; for I am with thee — one of the most reassuring and memorized verses in all scripture.
Behold my servant whom I uphold — the first formal introduction of the Servant figure.
A bruised reed shall he not break — describing the Servant's gentle mission.
When thou passest through the waters, I will be with thee — a promise of divine companionship through trials.
I, even I, am he that blotteth out thy transgressions for mine own sake.
I will pour my spirit upon thy seed — a promise of the Spirit being poured out on Israel's posterity.
Every knee shall bow, every tongue shall swear — a declaration of universal acknowledgment of God.
Declaring the end from the beginning — God's omniscience and sovereign plan.
O that thou hadst hearkened; then had thy peace been as a river — a lament over disobedience.
Can a woman forget her sucking child? Yet will I not forget thee — God's unfailing love for Zion.
I have graven thee upon the palms of my hands — an atonement foreshadowing.
I have set my face like a flint — the Servant's unwavering resolve to fulfill His mission.
The redeemed of the Lord shall return and come with singing unto Zion; everlasting joy shall be upon their head.
How beautiful upon the mountains are the feet of him that bringeth good tidings, that publisheth peace.
He is despised and rejected of men; a man of sorrows, and acquainted with grief.
He was wounded for our transgressions, he was bruised for our iniquities; with his stripes we are healed.
All we like sheep have gone astray; and the Lord hath laid on him the iniquity of us all.
The mountains shall depart, but my kindness shall not depart from thee — God's everlasting covenant of peace.
Ho, every one that thirsteth, come ye to the waters — a universal invitation of grace.
My thoughts are not your thoughts, neither are your ways my ways — divine transcendence.
Mine house shall be called an house of prayer for all people — quoted by Jesus when cleansing the temple.
I dwell in the high and holy place, with him also that is of a contrite and humble spirit.
To loose the bands of wickedness, to let the oppressed go free — the true fast that God has chosen.
The Lord shall guide thee continually and make thy bones fat — blessings of true fasting.
Your iniquities have separated between you and your God — the foundational statement on sin's effect.
Arise, shine; for thy light is come, and the glory of the Lord is risen upon thee.
The Spirit of the Lord God is upon me — the passage Jesus read in the Nazareth synagogue.
To give the oil of joy for mourning, the garment of praise for the spirit of heaviness.
Thou shalt no more be termed Forsaken; but Hephzi-bah and Beulah — new names of honor for Zion.
In all their affliction he was afflicted, and the angel of his presence saved them.
Eye hath not seen, nor ear heard, what God hath prepared for him that waiteth — quoted by Paul.
But now, O Lord, thou art our father; we are the clay, and thou our potter.
Behold, I create new heavens and a new earth — the vision of millennial renewal.
The wolf and the lamb shall feed together — a vision of paradisiacal peace.
The heaven is my throne, and the earth is my footstool — God's transcendence over any earthly temple.
To this man will I look, even to him that is poor and of a contrite spirit.
